comment_text.sn4.1:1.1: The first five discourses of this chapter are set shortly after the Buddha’s awakening, and hence in the period of time covered in the first chapter of the Vinaya Khandhaka (Kd 1). However, despite the length of that narrative, only the fourth and fifth are included there. This situation mirrors the Mahāparinibbānasutta, where there are also events missing from the narrative as we have it. Either parts were lost, or the compilers left out some events for some reason.
comment_text.sn4.1:1.2: In modern times this is the pilgrimage site Bodhgaya in the Gaya district of Bihar. It lies by a wide river that is dry in summer but abundant in the rains. In Pali the river is called Nerañjarā, which  is of unclear meaning, but the commentary suggests “sweet waters” (nela-jala) or “blue waters” (nīla-jala). The Sanskrit is nairañjanā, which suggests the sense “unblemished”. Today it is called the Līlajan (or Nīlajan). | Uruvelā, which aptly means “Broadbanks”, was a locality centered around the market town of Senānigama (“Marshalltown”). There was also a sizable hermitage nearby with matted-hair Brahmanical ascetics (jaṭilā) who ritually bathed in the water (Kd 1:20.15.1). After teaching the five monks in Varanasi, the Buddha returned here to teach them, but there is no record of him visiting subsequently.
comment_text.sn4.1:1.4: “Grueling work” (dukkarakārikā) refers to the self-mortification he practiced for six years before awakening (MN 12:56.1, MN 100:27.5), which was similar to Jain practices (MN 14:17.5).
comment_text.sn4.1:1.6: I follow the MS reading here. Sinhalese editions have “steady, mindful” instead of “freed”. Commentary is silent.
comment_text.sn4.1:2.1: Māra is sometimes called Namuci, and in fact seems to descend from the Vedic adversary of that name. Vedic Namuci “the wicked” (pāpmā vai namuciḥ, Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa 12.7.3, Maitrāyaṇī Saṁhitā 4.4.4) was a wily trickster (māyin, Rig Veda 1.53.7c) who was nonetheless outsmarted by Indra (Rig Veda 5.30.6c), and possessed ineffectual armies reliant on women (Rig Veda 5.30.9a). Buddhist Māra “the wicked” (māro pāpimā) was a wily trickster (passim) who was nonetheless outsmarted by the Buddha and his followers (passim); he possessed ineffectual armies (DN 20:21.3, AN 4.13:2.3, Snp 3.2), and sent his daughters to do his dirty work (SN 4.25). The decapitated head of Namuci introduced death (mṛtyu) in the form of blood to the deathless (amṛta) soma (Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa 12.7.3.4, Maitrāyaṇī Saṁhitā 4.4.4), establishing his Pali names Maccurāja, “King of Death” and Māra, “Death”. He appears in a similar guise in Jainism too (Ācaraṅga 1.3.1.3).
comment_text.sn4.1:3.1: Māra pretends to favor asceticism. Truth means nothing to him, as words are merely devices for him to get what he wants.
comment_text.sn4.1:5.2: The split compound amaraṁ tapaṁ also occurs at  Thag 3.1:1.4, and at Snp 2.2:11.4 we have amarā bahū tapā. The commentary explains that amara here is “immortality”, and the phrase means “fervent mortification aimed at immortality”. However, I find this unconvincing, one reason being that amara is an adjective (and thus must be glossed with an abstract noun in dative, amarabhāvatthāya). It is simpler to assume Pali has an idiomatic sense of amara as “endless”, which occurs here, in the phrase amarāvikkhepa (DN 1:2.23.1), and in the Niddesa’s amarāvitakka (Cnd 12:6.1). Then amarā and bahū are synonyms. I cannot find any use in Sanskrit that parallels this, but amara is noted by Edgerton in Buddhist Hybrid Sanskrit as “a high number”.
comment_text.sn4.1:5.4: Phiyāritta is the fittings for a boat at AN 4.196:8.12. Phiya is perhaps Hybrid Sanskrit sphija, “broom”. Aritta is Sanskrit aritra, “oar, rudder”. | Dhammani (Vedic dhanvan, “desert”) is the neuter noun dhamman in locative singular.
comment_text.sn4.1:6.4: By calling Māra “terminator”, the Buddha makes plain the threat implicit in his name.

root_text.sn4.1:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā uruvelāyaṁ viharati najjā nerañjarāya tīre ajapālanigrodhamūle paṭhamābhisambuddho.
root_text.sn4.1:1.3: Atha kho bhagavato rahogatassa paṭisallīnassa evaṁ cetaso parivitakko udapādi:
root_text.sn4.1:1.5: Sādhu mutto vatamhi tāya anatthasaṁhitāya dukkarakārikāya.
root_text.sn4.1:2.1: Atha kho māro pāpimā bhagavato cetasā cetoparivitakkamaññāya yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ gāthāya ajjhabhāsi:
root_text.sn4.1:4.1: Atha kho bhagavā “māro ayaṁ pāpimā” iti viditvā māraṁ pāpimantaṁ gāthāhi ajjhabhāsi:
root_text.sn4.1:7.1: Atha kho māro pāpimā “jānāti maṁ bhagavā, jānāti maṁ sugato”ti, dukkhī dummano tatthevantaradhāyīti.

translation_text.sn4.1:1.2: At one time, when he was first awakened, the Buddha was staying in Uruvelā at the root of the goatherd’s banyan tree on the bank of the Nerañjarā River.
translation_text.sn4.1:1.3: Then as he was in private retreat this thought came to his mind,
translation_text.sn4.1:1.4: “I am truly freed from that grueling work!
translation_text.sn4.1:1.5: Thank goodness I’m freed from that pointless grueling work.
translation_text.sn4.1:1.6: Thank goodness I’m freed and have attained awakening.”
translation_text.sn4.1:2.1: And then Māra the Wicked, knowing the Buddha’s train of thought, went up to him and addressed him in verse:
translation_text.sn4.1:3.2: by which sons of men purify themselves.
translation_text.sn4.1:3.3: You’re impure, but think yourself pure;
translation_text.sn4.1:3.4: you’ve strayed from the path of purity.”
translation_text.sn4.1:4.1: Then the Buddha, knowing that this was Māra the Wicked, replied to him in verse:
translation_text.sn4.1:5.4: as oars and rudder on dry land.
translation_text.sn4.1:6.2: by developing this path to awakening
translation_text.sn4.1:7.1: Then Māra the Wicked, thinking, “The Buddha knows me! The Holy One knows me!” miserable and sad, vanished right there.

variant_text.sn4.1:1.6: Sādhu vatamhi mutto bodhiṁ samajjhagan”ti → sādhu ṭhito sato bodhiṁ samajjhaganti (bj, pts1ed, pts2ed); sādhu vatamhi satto bodhisamajjhagūti (sya-all, km)
variant_text.sn4.1:3.4: suddhimaggā aparaddho → suddhimaggamaparaddho (bj, pts1ed, pts2ed); suddhimaggaṁ aparaddho (sya-all, km)
variant_text.sn4.1:5.2: amaraṁ tapaṁ → aparaṁ tapaṁ (sya-all, pts1ed, pts2ed, mr)
